class_schedule.topic: The Rhetorical Situation: Audience, Purpose, and Context
learning_objectives: Analyze writing situations using the rhetorical triangle: writer, audience, subject
Identify how purpose and context shape writing choices at every level
Distinguish between writing for academic, professional, and public audiences
readings: Lunsford, Everything's an Argument, Ch. 1 - 'Understanding Arguments'

lecture_notes.introduction: Advanced composition is about making deliberate choices. Every sentence you write is shaped by who you are writing for, why you are writing, and what context surrounds the communication. This course develops your ability to analyze those factors and write accordingly.
key_concepts: The rhetorical triangle: ethos (credibility), pathos (emotion), logos (logic)
Audience analysis: what does the reader already know, believe, and care about?
Purpose types: to inform, to persuade, to analyze, to propose
Register and tone: matching language to situation
Orwell's rules for clear writing and their ongoing relevance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing three versions of the same message — a scientific abstract, a newspaper article, and a tweet — to identify how audience and purpose transform the content
setup_requirements: Course textbook and access to LMS

review.value: The writer's credibility and authority, established through expertise, fairness, and trustworthiness
The timeliness and appropriateness of an argument — the right message at the right moment for the right audience

class_schedule.topic: Argument and Evidence: Building a Case
learning_objectives: Construct a thesis that makes a debatable claim rather than stating a fact or opinion
Identify and deploy different types of evidence: statistical, anecdotal, expert testimony, textual
Recognize and avoid logical fallacies in argumentative writing
readings: Lunsford, Everything's an Argument, Ch. 4 - 'Arguments Based on Facts and Reason'

lecture_notes.introduction: An argument is not a fight. It is a structured attempt to persuade a reasonable person by presenting evidence and reasoning. The quality of your evidence and the soundness of your logic determine whether your argument succeeds.
key_concepts: Claims, reasons, and evidence: the architecture of argument
Types of evidence and their relative strengths in different contexts
Logical fallacies: ad hominem, straw man, false dichotomy, hasty generalization
Counterargument and concession: acknowledging the strongest opposing view
The Toulmin model: claim, data, warrant, backing, qualifier, rebuttal
lecture_notes.demonstration: Dissecting a published op-ed to identify its claim, evidence, warrants, and handling of counterarguments
setup_requirements: Printed copy of the assigned op-ed

review.value: The often unstated assumption that connects evidence to a claim — the reason the evidence actually supports the conclusion
Addressing the strongest objection to your argument strengthens your credibility by showing you have considered alternatives honestly

learning_objectives: Navigate academic databases to find peer-reviewed sources efficiently
Evaluate sources for credibility, relevance, and bias using the CRAAP test
Synthesize multiple sources into a coherent literature review rather than a source-by-source summary
readings: Booth et al., The Craft of Research, Ch. 5-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Research is not googling. Academic research requires systematic searching, rigorous evaluation, and synthesis — bringing sources into conversation with each other and with your own argument.
key_concepts: Database search strategies: keywords, Boolean operators, subject headings
The CRAAP test: Currency, Relevance, Authority, Accuracy, Purpose
Primary vs. secondary vs. tertiary sources
Synthesis vs. summary: sources talk to each other, not just to you
Annotated bibliography as a research planning tool
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live database search demonstration followed by collaborative evaluation of three sources on the same topic using the CRAAP criteria

review.value: Combining ideas from multiple sources to identify patterns, contradictions, and gaps rather than summarizing each source separately
A framework for evaluating sources: Currency (when published), Relevance (to your topic), Authority (author credentials), Accuracy (evidence/peer review), Purpose (why written)

class_schedule.topic: Prose Style: Clarity, Concision, and Coherence
learning_objectives: Identify and eliminate common sources of unclear writing: nominalizations, passive voice overuse, and unnecessary qualifiers
Apply principles of sentence-level coherence: known-to-new information flow
Vary sentence structure for rhythm and emphasis
readings: Williams and Bizup, Style: Lessons in Clarity and Grace, Ch. 3-5
Strunk and White, The Elements of Style, Ch. 2 (review)

lecture_notes.introduction: Clear writing is not dumbed-down writing. It is writing where the reader can follow your thought without unnecessary friction. Clarity is a courtesy to your reader and a sign of clear thinking.
key_concepts: Nominalizations: turning verbs and adjectives into nouns weakens sentences
Active vs. passive voice: when each is appropriate
Known-new contract: sentences flow when old information comes first and new information comes last
Sentence variety: mixing short and long sentences for rhythm and emphasis
Cutting: most first drafts can lose 20% of their words and improve
lecture_notes.demonstration: Sentence-level revision workshop: students revise a paragraph of deliberately murky academic prose into clear, direct writing

review.value: Turning a verb or adjective into a noun ('investigate' becomes 'investigation'), which hides the action and often forces passive constructions
Readers follow prose most easily when each sentence begins with familiar information (known) and ends with the new point being made

class_schedule.topic: Writing Across Genres: Analysis, Argument, Proposal
learning_objectives: Identify the conventions and expectations of analytical, argumentative, and proposal genres
Adapt writing strategies to match the requirements of different genres
Analyze how genre shapes the organization, evidence, and tone of a piece
readings: Sample analytical essay, argumentative essay, and proposal (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Different writing situations demand different genres. An analytical essay explains how something works; an argument persuades the reader to accept a position; a proposal convinces someone to act. Knowing the conventions of each genre is essential to writing effectively within it.
key_concepts: Analytical writing: explains how or why without advocating a position
Argumentative writing: takes a position and defends it with evidence and reasoning
Proposal writing: identifies a problem and argues for a specific solution
Genre conventions: structure, evidence types, tone, and reader expectations
Audience adaptation: how the same topic changes across genres
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing three texts about the same issue — a literary analysis, an op-ed argument, and a policy proposal — to identify how genre shapes every aspect of the writing
setup_requirements: Three sample texts printed for comparison

review.value: The shared expectations of writers and readers within a type of writing — knowing the conventions allows you to meet or deliberately break them for effect

Problem definition, proposed solution, justification with evidence, implementation plan, and anticipated objections — the reader must be convinced both that the problem is real and that the solution is feasible

learning_objectives: Provide specific, actionable peer review feedback on drafts-in-progress
Apply a systematic self-editing process to your own writing
Distinguish between higher-order concerns (argument, structure) and lower-order concerns (grammar, formatting)
readings: Bean, Engaging Ideas, Ch. 15 - 'Coaching the Writing Process'
Peer review rubric and guidelines (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Good writers are good revisers, and the ability to give and receive feedback is a professional skill that extends far beyond the classroom. Today we practice both.
key_concepts: Higher-order concerns: thesis clarity, argument structure, evidence quality, audience awareness
Lower-order concerns: grammar, punctuation, formatting, citation mechanics
The feedback hierarchy: address higher-order concerns first
Descriptive feedback: tell the writer what you see happening in the text
Self-editing checklist: a systematic process for revising your own work
lecture_notes.demonstration: Structured peer review workshop: students exchange drafts and provide written feedback using a guided rubric, then discuss the feedback in pairs
setup_requirements: Draft of current essay for peer review

review.value: Telling the writer 'I see you making three arguments here, but only the second one has evidence' is more useful than 'This needs work'
After drafting, write a one-sentence summary of each paragraph's main point; this reveals structural problems, repetition, and gaps in the argument

questions.question: Identify and explain one logical fallacy in the following argument: 'We should not listen to the professor's views on climate policy because she drives an SUV.'
questions.answer: This is an ad hominem fallacy. It attacks the person making the argument rather than addressing the argument itself. The professor's personal vehicle choice does not invalidate her expertise or the evidence behind her climate policy views.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the fallacy as ad hominem
Explains why the personal attack is irrelevant to the argument's validity

questions.question: What is a 'warrant' in the Toulmin model of argument? Provide an example using a claim and evidence of your choice.
questions.answer: A warrant is the underlying assumption that connects evidence to a claim. Example — Claim: 'The university should extend library hours.' Evidence: 'Student surveys show 70% want later hours.' Warrant: 'University policy should reflect the needs expressed by the majority of students.' The warrant is often unstated but must be defensible.

rubric.key: Correct definition of warrant as the connecting assumption
Example includes a clear claim, evidence, and warrant
Notes that warrants are often unstated

questions.question: Revise the following sentence to eliminate the nominalization and strengthen the verb: 'The implementation of the new policy was carried out by the administration.'
questions.answer: 'The administration implemented the new policy.' This revision replaces the nominalization ('implementation') with the active verb ('implemented'), eliminates the passive voice, and reduces the sentence from 13 words to 7 without losing meaning.

rubric.key: Revised sentence uses active voice with a strong verb
Meaning is preserved while clarity improves

questions.question: How does the structure of a proposal differ from the structure of an analytical essay? Name two specific structural differences.
questions.answer: First, a proposal must include a problem definition section that establishes the need for action, while an analytical essay begins with a question or puzzle to investigate. Second, a proposal must include an implementation plan with specific, actionable steps, while an analytical essay concludes with implications or further questions rather than a call to action.

rubric.key: First structural difference is accurate and specific
Second structural difference is accurate and specific
Differences reflect genuine genre knowledge, not surface observations

major_assignments.title: Researched Argumentative Essay
major_assignments.description: Write a 2,000-2,500 word argumentative essay on a topic of your choice that takes a clear position and defends it using at least five credible sources. The essay must address counterarguments and demonstrate command of rhetorical strategies.
objectives: Construct a clear, debatable thesis supported by researched evidence
Integrate sources through quotation, paraphrase, and summary with proper citation
Address and refute at least one significant counterargument
Demonstrate awareness of audience and rhetorical situation
deliverables: 2,000-2,500 word argumentative essay in MLA format
Works Cited page with at least 5 credible sources
Annotated bibliography for all sources (submitted separately)
Reverse outline of the final draft demonstrating structural logic

criteria: Thesis is clear, specific, and debatable
Argument develops logically with well-supported reasons
Counterargument is addressed fairly and refuted effectively
Conclusion extends the argument rather than merely summarizing
Sources are credible, relevant, and varied in type
Sources are integrated smoothly, not dumped in block quotations
Student's voice and analysis dominate the essay, not the sources
All citations follow MLA format accurately
Prose is clear, concise, and free of errors
Sentence variety and strong verbs create readable, engaging prose
Transitions connect paragraphs and sections logically
Tone is appropriate for an academic audience
Strong, well-supported argument with a debatable thesis
Effective integration of credible research sources
Fair and substantive engagement with counterarguments
Clear, polished prose appropriate for an academic audience

argumentative_essay.description: Researched argumentative essay demonstrating evidence-based persuasion

analytical_essay.description: Analytical essay demonstrating explanation of how a text or phenomenon works

proposal.description: Formal proposal identifying a problem and arguing for a specific solution

quizzes_and_exercises.description: Quizzes on rhetorical concepts and in-class style revision exercises

peer_review_and_participation.description: Quality of peer review feedback, class participation, and revision portfolio

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory, particularly on peer review days. More than 3 unexcused absences result in a full letter grade deduction. Peer review absences cannot be made up.
course_policies.late_submissions: Major essays are penalized 5% per day late. Peer review drafts must be submitted on time; failure to have a draft ready for peer review results in a zero for that peer review session.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review is collaborative by design. All written work submitted for a grade must be your own. You may discuss ideas with classmates but must write independently.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Plagiarism, including uncited paraphrase, patchwriting, and submission of AI-generated text as your own, will result in a zero on the assignment and referral to the academic integrity board.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Laptop with word processing software, university library database access, and LMS access for submissions and course materials.
